data.title: Predicting Market Supercycles
data.description:
Author: Tulip King; Source: X, @0xTulipKing; Translated by: Shaw Jinse Finance

Prediction markets are not yet fully understood, and that's your opportunity. We are witnessing a complete reshaping of how markets work, how information is priced, and most importantly—how the future is shaped. If you ignore this, you will miss the biggest trading opportunity in the market since the digitalization of options.

The iPhone Moment

In every technological revolution, there is a wonderful period when people are unable to perceive disruptive changes because they are stuck in old ways of thinking. In 2007, Nokia executives looked at the iPhone and said, "It doesn't even have a keyboard." They compared it to a mobile phone, ignoring the fact that it should be compared to a computer. The iPhone is not competing with existing mobile phones—it has revolutionized the concept of a single-function device.

This is exactly what is happening in prediction markets today.

People saw Polymarket and thought it was some strange, illiquid betting site. They compared it to DraftKings (a sports betting platform) or the Chicago Mercantile Exchange (CME) and found it inferior. They made a Nokia-esque mistake. Polymarket isn't a better betting site—it's replacing the entire concept of a professional financial market. Consider this: when you strip away all the complexities, what is the essence of each financial instrument? Options: betting "yes" or "no" on price levels; Insurance: betting "yes" or "no" on the occurrence of a disaster; Credit Default Swaps: betting "yes" or "no" on bankruptcy; Sports Betting: betting "yes" or "no" on the outcome of a sporting event. We've built trillion-dollar industries around fundamentally binary questions, each with its own infrastructure, regulatory systems, and monopolistic intermediaries from which it extracts profits. Polymarket simplifies all of this into one fundamental element: creating markets for any observable event, allowing people to trade, and settling accounts when reality determines the outcome. It's not better than DraftKings at sports betting, nor better than CME at derivatives. It does something more fundamental—simplifying all markets to their most basic units and rebuilding them from there. Polymarket is like the iPhone; everything else is just an app. Multi-dimensional Trading When all trading takes place in the same venue, possibilities that didn't exist before are unlocked. Imagine five years ago, if you wanted to construct a position expressing the view: "I think the Fed will raise rates, but tech stocks will rise anyway because Trump will tweet some positive news about artificial intelligence." You would need accounts at different financial institutions, dealing with different regulatory frameworks, and using different leverage. And the "Trump mentions" trade simply didn't exist in the market back then. On Polymarket, this can be done with just three clicks. More importantly, these aren't three separate bets, but rather a coherent worldview expressed through interconnected positions. You could buy a short position on "the Fed pauses rate hikes," a long position on "the Nasdaq hits a record high," and a long position on "Trump will mention artificial intelligence in his next speech." This correlation itself is the key trade.

For those who are still following, here's an example. Last month on Polymarket, you could build a position like this: buy a position at 67 cents on "Hyperliquid won't airdrop before December 31st," and at the same time buy a position at 13 cents on "Will Hyperliquid fall to $20 by 2026?". Consider this outcome matrix: **Maximum Profit Scenario:** Hyperliquid doesn't airdrop, and the price drops to $20 this year. Given the current market conditions, this seems like a reasonable assumption, and the price should be 8% higher than the current market value. You are buying this outcome at a low price. **Minor Profit Scenario:** Hyperliquid airdrops, causing the price to drop to $20, or the price drops to $20 without an airdrop. These two scenarios are the most likely, with a probability of 63%. **Full Loss Scenario:** Hyperliquid airdrops, and the price remains above $20. Given the market's significant concerns about the new supply from team unlocks, it seems that airdrops introducing more supply would be met with a very negative reaction. The probability of this outcome is less than 29%, so you're selling this outcome at a high price. This sounds like hedging, but it's much more than that. It expresses a complex view of how the market handles new token supply, a view that's difficult to articulate accurately anywhere else. Traditional markets force you to compress complex ideas into rough directional bets. You might have a complex argument that Nvidia's (NVDA) earnings will exceed expectations, but it will still fall because market expectations are ahead of reality. In the options market, you can only choose between call and put options, perhaps constructing a spread that barely expresses your meaning but is costly. On the Polymarket, you only need to buy the options "Nvidia earnings exceed expectations" and "Nvidia stock price drops 5% after earnings release" to accurately express your view. The real brilliance lies in what you do when you start thinking about cross-market connections that shouldn't exist but do. For example, a hurricane is approaching Florida, while the Tampa Bay Buccaneers are playing in Detroit. Conventional wisdom considers these two events unrelated. But you have an assumption: if the hurricane does hit Florida, the NFL referees will likely favor the Buccaneers, creating a compelling narrative. So, you bet 60 cents on the Buccaneers losing and 20 cents on the hurricane hitting Florida. You're not betting on a particular outcome, but on a connection structure. You profit from understanding how narratives influence referee decisions. This is what I mean when I say prediction markets don't compete with existing markets—they operate on a completely different level of abstraction. All other markets offer only one option, while a polymarket offers an infinite number of options, and more importantly, it allows you to choose specific combinations of options based on your understanding of how the world works. Why are the "savvy" people wrong (again)? The first criticism you hear about prediction markets is always about liquidity. "Oh, you can't trade at scale." "The spreads are too big." "It's just idiots gambling with their lunch money." But that's not a loophole—that's your opportunity.

Think about the reasons for the impending liquidity explosion from a mechanistic perspective. Traditional market making is relatively simple—you usually make markets on things that have a clear correlation with other things. Stock options are correlated with stock prices, futures are correlated with spot prices. Each thing has hedging, correlation, and models that can be relied upon. This is why a few companies like Citadel and Jane Street are able to make markets for thousands of financial instruments.

Predicting the market is more difficult.

Each market type requires its own specialized intelligence: Sports markets need models that can be updated with every score, every injury report, and every weather change. Political markets require natural language processing to analyze polls, speeches, and social media sentiment. Event markets need machine learning systems that can calculate basic probabilities based on historical data. Mention markets require language models trained on thousands of text records. You can't let one market maker monopolize all markets because each market requires completely different expertise. In the long run, this actually benefits liquidity. We will no longer see a few giants monopolizing all market-making businesses, but rather an explosive growth in professional market makers. Some quantitative analyst teams will become the world's best experts at pricing mentions in the market. Other teams will dominate weather-related events. Still others will focus on celebrity behavior. This seemingly weak fragmentation will actually create resilience and depth. In the next five years, we will see a completely new type of financial company emerge—prediction market experts. They won't be making markets in the stock or bond markets, but in reality itself. And the companies that first achieve large-scale success in this will become the bastions of the new financial system. The Disturbing Facts About Truth Now let's put forward some real arguments: prediction markets aren't about prediction itself; they're about creating a financial incentive for truth. We live in a bizarre age where everyone has an opinion on everything, but nobody is really taking risks. Your favorite Twitter analyst has predicted twelve recessions that have only occurred twice in the past. CNBC commentators have botched more accounts than Do Kwon. Yet they retain their platforms, their audiences, and continue making incorrect predictions without consequence. This model is unsustainable, and everyone knows that deep down. The information ecosystem we've built rewards engagement, not accuracy; volume matters more than correctness. Social media has exacerbated this problem—the most popular opinions now prevail, regardless of their accuracy. Those with the most followers become experts, and so-called experts are simply those who get the most likes. Prediction markets have completely disrupted this model. Suddenly, there's a price to pay for both correct and incorrect predictions. The market doesn't care if you graduated from Harvard, have a verified account, or have written books on marketing. It only cares if your predictions are correct. When you build a system that only rewards accuracy, something amazing happens: those who predict correctly suddenly have a reason to speak, while those who predict incorrectly finally have a reason to shut up. But this is not simply a matter of transferring wealth from those who shouldn't have it to those who should. Prediction markets are building a parallel information system whose operating mechanism is drastically different from our current media ecosystem. In the old world: information spreads virally on social networks. In prediction markets: information is priced according to reality. These are fundamentally different selection mechanisms, and therefore produce drastically different results. We have been observing this phenomenon in real time on Polymarket. The predictive power of the political market has surpassed any aggregated poll data. Markets related to the Federal Reserve fluctuate before any economists update their forecasts. The yield market has already priced in results that won't appear in analyst reports for weeks. This is not because traders in the prediction market are smarter, but because their incentives are to ensure accurate predictions, not to chase hype.

Bounty Markets, Not Prediction Markets

Now things are getting really strange, and I think everyone's overlooking the most important dynamic. We keep calling these "prediction markets," but that's like calling Bitcoin "digital gold"—technically correct, but completely ignoring something more important. True prediction markets are passive observers. They price probabilities but don't influence the outcome. Weather futures markets don't change the weather. But polymarkets aren't passive, and that's the key: **any market with human participants contains the potential to change the outcome.**

Let me explain what I mean. Someone placed a bet on whether someone would throw a green object during a WNBA game, and someone calculated the odds:

  1. Buying $10,000 worth of YES at 15 cents per bet.

  2. buying $10,000 worth of YES at 15 cents per bet.

  4. Throw the item yourself.

  5. When the market result is "YES", you receive $66,000.

  6. Net profit after deducting legal fees and lifetime ban: approximately $50,000

The equilibrium dynamics here are very interesting. Theoretically, the WNBA disorderly conduct bounty should be set at exactly this price:

Compensation Amount = Criminal Charges + Social Shame + Lifetime Ban + Required Effort

If the price is too high, it will attract imitators; if the price is too low, no one will pay attention. The market will find the optimal equilibrium price.

The "prediction market" has become a bounty market—it no longer predicts whether someone will throw something, but instead provides a specific amount of money for someone to make that happen. This isn't a loophole, nor is it manipulation; it's the most important characteristic of prediction markets, yet no one mentions it.

Consider this thought experiment. I decide to run for mayor of New York City. The market gives me a fairly reasonable chance of winning, only 0.5%. Based on this probability, I could spend $100,000 to buy 20 bets, each worth $5,000. If I win, each bet would bring in a staggering $1 million.

... What's even more interesting is that I used these betting shares as rewards for my campaign team. I hired 20 hitmen and promised each of them $1 million if we ultimately won. I just created something that shouldn't exist: a market-funded political campaign where my leverage increased as the odds of winning decreased. The market was essentially saying, "This outcome is too unlikely, so we're giving you 20 to 1 odds to make it happen." Prediction became a bounty. The market wasn't just observing reality—it was also funding specific versions of the future. Some events are completely unaffected by bounty mechanisms. For example, the elected president is inherently valuable, and no bounty from any prediction market could significantly increase its appeal. But for thousands of other events—from corporate decisions to cultural phenomena to sporting events—this bounty mechanism is real and in effect. We haven't arrived at the kind of future politics envisioned by Robin Hansen, where prediction markets guide governance through wisdom. What we get is something even stranger: the market pays people to make specific future events happen. Conclusion: My prediction is that within ten years, the Polymarket model will devour most of the traditional financial system. This is not only because it's a lower-cost, better-experience trading venue, but also because it integrates all markets into a single foundational platform and rebuilds upon it, which is fundamentally far more efficient than maintaining thousands of specialized market structures. The dominoes will fall in the following order: DraftKings—Sports betting is essentially a lower-odds prediction market. CBOE—Options are essentially complex binary bets on price levels. Insurance—merely a prediction market, where only one party can trade. Credit Markets—bankruptcy prediction (with additional steps) Every vertical will resist, will regulate, but will eventually yield because they will realize they are not competing with better products—but with better principles. What we are truly witnessing now is a complete restructuring of how markets operate. You no longer bet on prices, interest rates, or volatility, but on events, the correlations between events, and most importantly, the probability of changing events. Savvy investors should prepare for this now. This is not just about buying tokens or trading markets, but about thinking about what will happen when any observable event can have an active market. What will happen when every corporate decision has a prediction market? What will happen when every piece of legislation has odds? What happens when every cultural trend has a price? What happens when entrepreneurs can raise funds by selling shares of their seemingly impossible ventures? What happens when anyone with confidence in the future can leverage that confidence? We're not just building better markets; we're building a mechanism that incentivizes people to create the future.

data.title: Do Kwon Faces 40-Year Threat in South Korea as Defense Seeks 5-Year Cap in U.S.
data.description:
Do Kwon Pleads For His Prison Term To Be Capped At Five Years

Terraform Labs co-founder Do Kwon is fighting a legal battle on two fronts as he is begging for U.S. prosecutors to limit his prison term to five years as prosecutors in South Korea are weighing a potential 40-year sentence over his role in the collapse of the Terra ecosystem.

His legal team argues that extending his U.S. punishment beyond five years would be excessive, given the harsh punishment that is awaiting him at home.

🚨 LATEST: Do Kwon has asked a U.S. judge to limit his sentence to five years for the Terra collapse, even as he faces up to 40 years in a separate case in South Korea. pic.twitter.com/COdpaX9o8u

— Cointelegraph (@Cointelegraph) November 27, 2025

Kwon is urging the Manhattan federal court to adopt a five-year sentence, which he frames as proportionate given his nearly three years behind bars — including what his lawyers describe as “severe, punitive conditions” in Montenegro.

The defense argues that a sentence longer than five years would be “far greater than necessary,” especially since he still faces potential decades-long punishment in South Korea.

Under Kwon’s plea agreement, U.S. prosecutors committed not to seek more than 12 years. However, the presiding judge retains discretion to impose up to 25 years, leaving the final outcome uncertain.

Following his extradition from Montenegro, Kwon pleaded guilty in August to two counts of wire fraud and conspiracy to defraud in connection with Terra’s catastrophic $40 billion collapse in 2022. His team highlights that he has already endured significant personal and financial consequences, noting the years he has spent in custody and the global reputational fallout.

Do Kwon arrested in Montenegro.

What did he think he was a Bond villain? This place is too nice to hide out. pic.twitter.com/IoFSm1ojio

— HORSE 🏴‍☠️ (@TheFlowHorse) March 23, 2023

The plea deal also requires Kwon to forfeit more than $19 million in assets, including multiple properties — adding a heavy financial layer to the criminal penalties.

South Korean Prosecution: A Potential 40-Year Sentence Still Awaits

Even after the U.S. case concludes, Kwon’s legal problems will intensify. South Korean prosecutors are preparing their own criminal sentencing recommendation, reportedly eyeing up to 40 years in prison for his role in the Terra collapse — a punishment reflecting the scale of losses to local retail investors and the broader impact on the country’s crypto markets.

So $LUNA Do Kwon is asking a US judge to lower his prison term to 5 YEARS, while South Korean prosecutors are pursuing up to 40 YEARS in a separate case.

I’d rather see all his wallets and TFL’s $LUNC wallets burned. Let’s see that. 💎🤲🏻 #Cryptopic.twitter.com/a3pxjftvuc

— Mr. Diamondhandz1💎 (@MrDiamondhandz1) November 28, 2025

This looming prosecution is central to Kwon’s argument for leniency in the U.S., with his defense stressing that any lengthy American sentence would stack atop a potentially decades-long term in Seoul, compounding his total time behind bars.

U.S. District Judge Paul Engelmayer is scheduled to sentence Kwon on Dec. 11, with prosecutors expected to file their recommendation days before the hearing. Kwon’s legal saga began after months on the run following the Terra crash, before he was arrested in Montenegro for traveling with falsified documents. He served four months there before being extradited to the United States.

Kwon’s case now sits alongside other major crypto criminal prosecutions — including that of former FTX CEO Sam Bankman-Fried, who is currently appealing his 25-year U.S. sentence.

data.title: Dragonfly Partner: Why are new public blockchains met with criticism as soon as they are launched?
data.description:
Author: Haseeb, Managing Partner of Dragonfly; Translator: @Jinse Finance xz

I used to warn entrepreneurs: the feedback you get after your project launches won't be hatred, but indifference. By default, nobody cares about your new public chain.

But now I have to stop saying that. Monad just launched this week, and I've never seen a newly launched blockchain suffer so much hostility. I've been investing in the crypto space for over seven years. Before 2023, almost every new public chain I saw received either enthusiastic or indifferent feedback. But now, new public chains are accompanied by a barrage of criticism from the moment they're born. Projects like Monad, Tempo, and MegaETH even faced a lot of verbal attacks before their mainnet launch—this is indeed a new phenomenon.

I've been trying to understand: why is this happening now? What market psychology does it reveal?

I've been trying to understand: why is this happening now? What market psychology does it reveal?

...

1、The Cure is Worse Than the Disease

Disclaimer: This will be the least metrics-heavy blockchain valuation article you've ever read. I'm not trying to sell you any fancy metrics or charts. Instead, I'm going to refute the prevailing sentiment in the crypto Twitter world—one I've been on the opposite side of for the past few years.

Back in 2024, I felt like I was fighting against financial nihilism. This view held that all assets were irrelevant, ultimately just memes, and everything we built was essentially worthless.

Thankfully, that atmosphere has dissipated. We broke that spell.

But the prevailing sentiment now I call financial cynicism: Okay, maybe some things do have value, maybe not all of them are memes, but they're severely overvalued, and it's only a matter of time before Wall Street discovers the truth.

Not all public blockchains are worthless, but they might only be worth 1/5 to 1/10 of their current trading price (have you ever seen those P/E ratios?), so you'd better pray Wall Street doesn't expose our bluff, or everything will be for naught. Many bullish analysts are now trying to counter this sentiment by constructing optimistic layer-one network valuation models, inflating P/E ratios, gross margins, and discounted cash flow. Late last year, Solana proudly adopted REV as the metric that would finally justify its valuation. They proudly announced: We—and only we—are no longer bluffing Wall Street! However, almost immediately after REV was adopted, it plummeted (though it's worth noting that SOL outperformed REV).

It's not that the REV metric itself is problematic. It is indeed a very clever metric. But the focus of this article is not on metric selection.

Then Hyperliquid appeared. This decentralized exchange has real revenue, a buyback mechanism, and a P/E ratio. So everyone exclaimed—see, we told you so! Finally, the first token with real profits and a reasonable P/E ratio has appeared (don't mention BNB, we won't discuss it). Hyperliquid will devour everything because it's obvious that Ethereum and Solana aren't making money, and we no longer need to pretend they have valuations.

...

Hyperliquid, Pump, and Sky, these tokens that emphasize buybacks, are all excellent. But the market has always had a channel to invest in exchanges—you can always buy Coinbase stock, BNB, or other assets. We hold HYPE tokens and acknowledge that it's an excellent product.

But that's not the reason people invest in ETH and SOL. The fact that a single-layer network doesn't offer exchange-level profit margins is not their motivation for buying—if they were looking for profit, they could have bought Coinbase stock.

If you think this article isn't a critique of blockchain financial metrics, you might guess I'm condemning the original sin of the token industry complex.

Clearly, everyone has lost money on tokens over the past year, including venture capitalists. Altcoins have performed poorly this year. Therefore, the other half of the mainstream voice on crypto Twitter is debating who to blame—who got greedy? Was it the greed of venture capitalists? Wintermute? Binance? Farmers?

Or is it the founders' greed? The answer remains the same: everyone is greedy. Venture capitalists, Wintertermute, liquidity miners, Binance, KOLs, and even yourself—no one is exempt. But that doesn't matter, because a properly functioning market never requires anyone to act against their own interests. If our assessment of the crypto industry is correct, investments can still succeed even if everyone is greedy. Trying to analyze a declining market by identifying "greedy individuals" is futile, akin to conducting a witch trial. I dare say no one will suddenly become greedy in 2025. Therefore, this is not the topic of this article. Many people expect me to write an article arguing why MON should be valued at X or MEGA at Y. But I don't want to write such an article, nor do I advise you to buy any particular asset. In fact, if you don't believe in them, you probably shouldn't invest at all. Will the new chain challenge succeed? Nobody knows. However, if there is indeed a possibility of success, the market will price it accordingly. If Ethereum is worth $300 billion or Solana is worth $80 billion, then a project with a 1%-5% probability of becoming the next Ethereum or Solana will have its price reflect that expected probability. What's puzzling is that the crypto Twitter community is outraged by this, but it's no different from the biotechnology field. A drug with less than a 10% chance of curing Alzheimer's disease, even if there's a 90% chance it won't pass Phase III clinical trials and will become worthless, will still be valued at billions of dollars by the market. This is a manifestation of mathematical logic—the market is, as it turns out, very good at calculating. Binary results are based on probability pricing, not growth rates or moral flaws. This belongs to the valuation philosophy of the "shut up and calculate" school. I think discussing this issue is pointless. "5% chance of success? Impossible, that's clearly 10%!" For any individual token, the market, not the article, is the best evaluator. Therefore, what this article truly explores is: the crypto Twitter community seems to have lost faith in the value of public blockchains. I believe this isn't because they question the ability of new public blockchains to gain market share—we just witnessed Solana rise from the ashes and dominate the market in less than two years. This, while not easy, is certainly possible. The deeper reason lies in the growing belief that even if a new public blockchain wins, there's no worthwhile prize to fight for. If ETH is just a meme and will never generate real revenue, then even if you win, you won't achieve a $300 billion valuation. This race isn't worth participating in because all valuations are bubbles that will burst before you claim your reward. Optimism about public blockchain valuations seems out of place. It's not that no one is optimistic—obviously, there are bullish voices in the market. Where there's a seller, there's a buyer. Despite the crypto Twitter influencers' enthusiasm for predicting the downfall of layer one networks, some are still confidently buying SOL at $140 and ETH at $3,000. However, a prevailing belief is that the smartest people have already stopped buying smart contract public chains. The smart people know the trick has been exposed, either now or soon. Those buying now are just suckers—ride-hailing drivers, people like Tom Lee, and KOLs who constantly talk about "trillion-dollar market capitalization." Perhaps the US Treasury. But definitely not smart money. This is pure nonsense. I don't believe it, and you shouldn't either. Therefore, I feel it's necessary to write a "Manifesto of the Smart," explaining the value of general-purpose public chains. This article is not about Monad or MegaETH; it's actually a defense of ETH and SOL. Because if you accept the value of ETH and SOL, the rest of the reasoning falls into place.

Defending the valuations of ETH and SOL is not my job as a venture capitalist, but since no one else is willing to do it, I'll take the plunge.

2, Perceiving Exponential Growth

My partner, Bo, experienced the Chinese internet boom firsthand as a venture capitalist. Although I've become numb to the notion that "the crypto industry is like the internet," his stories always remind me of the high cost of misjudging trends.

He often tells a story: In the early 21st century, when all the early e-commerce venture capitalists (at the time, it was just a small circle) gathered for coffee, they debated the size of the e-commerce market.

Will it mainly sell electronics (perhaps only geeks use computers)? Can it attract female users (perhaps they value tactile experience more)? What about fresh food (perishable goods might be unmanageable)?

For early-stage venture capitalists, the answers to these questions directly determine investment direction and valuation logic. The result, of course, proved them all wildly wrong. E-commerce would sell everything, targeting customers worldwide. But at the time, nobody truly believed it, and even those who did dared not voice it because it seemed so absurd. Only by waiting long enough would exponential growth materialize. Even among those who believed in e-commerce at the time, very few thought it would reach its later scale. And those few who remained steadfast and held on became almost all billionaires. Other venture capitalists—who, according to Bo, were among them—sold out prematurely. Today, in the crypto space, believing in exponential growth is outdated. But I believe in the exponential law of the crypto world. Because I've experienced it firsthand. When I first entered the industry, nobody used these technologies: they were small-scale, riddled with bugs, and offered a terrible user experience. The total value locked on-chain is only a few million dollars. When we invested in first-generation DeFi projects—MakerDAO, Compound, and 1inch—they were still experimental products. I used EtherDelta, which had a daily trading volume of only a few million dollars, and that was considered a huge success at the time. Today, the daily trading volume on-chain routinely exceeds tens of billions of dollars. I used to think it was absurd that Tether's issuance exceeded 1 billion and it was reported by the New York Times as a Ponzi scheme on the verge of collapse; now stablecoins have a market capitalization of over $300 billion and are regulated by the Federal Reserve. I believe in exponential growth because I have witnessed it time and time again. But you might argue: stablecoin growth may be exponential, and so may DeFi trading volume, but the value hasn't been deposited into ETH or SOL. Public chains have failed to capture this value. To that, my answer is: you still don't believe in the exponential law. Because the answer to exponential growth remains the same: it doesn't matter. The future scale will far exceed today's imagination; when the great changes arrive, economies of scale will compensate for everything. See the image below. This is Amazon's profit and loss statement from 1995 to 2019, spanning 24 years. The red line represents revenue, and the gray line represents profit. See that small upward curve at the end of the gray line? That's when Amazon truly began to turn a profit in its 22nd year. When this gray line, representing net profit, first moved above zero, Amazon had already been established for 22 years. Every year prior, columnists, critics, and short sellers claimed Amazon was a Ponzi scheme that would never be profitable. Ethereum, meanwhile, had just turned 10. Here's how Amazon's stock performed over its first decade: A decade of turmoil. During this time, Amazon was constantly surrounded by skeptics and critics: Was e-commerce just a venture-funded charity? Who cared if they dumped cheap, low-quality goods on bargain hunters? How could they possibly make money like Walmart or General Electric? If you were arguing about Amazon's P/E ratio back then, you chose the wrong paradigm—it was the linear growth paradigm. But e-commerce isn't a linear trend, so everyone arguing about P/E ratios for the past 22 years has been wildly wrong. No matter how much you bid, no matter when you buy, your bullishness is far from sufficient. Because that's the law of exponential growth. For truly exponential technology, no matter how large you think it can reach, it will always exceed your imagination. This is precisely what Silicon Valley has always understood better than Wall Street. Silicon Valley grew through exponential growth, while Wall Street is steeped in linear thinking. In the past few years, the center of gravity of the crypto world has shifted from Silicon Valley to Wall Street—you can feel the change. Admittedly, crypto growth isn't as smooth as e-commerce growth. It's more explosive, with intermittent leaps. This is because crypto involves currency, is deeply tied to macro forces, and faces a more intense regulatory tug-of-war than e-commerce. Encryption strikes at the heart of a nation's economy—currency—making it more unsettling to governments than e-commerce. But the inevitability of exponential growth remains undiminished. This is a crude argument, but if encryption truly represents exponential growth, then this crude argument is correct. Zooming out, financial assets crave freedom. They crave openness. They crave interconnectivity. Encryption transforms financial assets into file formats, making sending dollars or stocks as simple as sending a PDF. It allows everything to communicate with each other, creating a 24/7, global, interconnected open ecosystem. Openness will ultimately prevail. This is a core principle I learned from the internet age. The vested interests will resist, governments will bluff, but ultimately they will succumb to the ubiquity, creativity, and extreme efficiency this technology brings. Just as the internet reshaped all industries, blockchain will similarly devour the entire financial and monetary system. Yes—given enough time—it will devour everything. There's an old saying: people always overestimate what will change in two years and underestimate what will change in ten. If you believe in exponential growth, if you broaden your perspective sufficiently, everything will still seem cheap. Every day, holders outlast sellers and detractors—this should keep you humble. Big capital has a much longer time horizon than the swing traders on crypto Twitter might convince you of. History has taught big capital not to fight against major technological changes. Remember that grand story that initially lured you into buying ETH or SOL? Big capital still believes in that story, and it never stops. So, what is my core argument? I argue that applying price-to-earnings ratios to smart contract public chains (the so-called "revenue meta-narrative") is essentially abandoning exponential growth. This means you've relegated the industry to a linear growth paradigm, that you believe 30 million daily active users and less than 1% of the total M2 money supply is the end—the crypto world is just one of countless industries, a marginal player. It hasn't won, nor is it inevitable. More importantly, I urge people to become its believers. Not just believers, but long-term believers. I firmly believe this exponential revolution will surpass any career you've ever been involved in. This is your "e-commerce revolution." When you're old and look back, you'll tell your children and grandchildren—I witnessed this upheaval. Not everyone believed that social systems could be restructured, or that programs running on our collectively owned decentralized computers could fundamentally change money and finance. But it did happen. It changed the world. And you were a part of it.

data.title: Arthur Hayes: Why are traditional financial derivatives all becoming perpetual contracts?
data.description:
Author: Arthur Hayes, Founder of BitMEX; Translated by: Jinse Finance

Less than a decade ago, those self-proclaimed high-flyers in traditional finance (TradFi) scoffed at BitMEX's 100x leveraged cryptocurrency derivatives market built using a new type of derivatives contract called perpetual contracts. When I read articles by Jim Grant of Grant's Interest Rate Observer and Dan Oliver of Myrmikan Capital pointing out that BitMEX offered such a "weapon of mass destruction" of cryptocurrency trading, I couldn't help but laugh. However, things have changed. Suddenly, traditional exchanges like the Singapore Exchange (SGX) and the Chicago Board Options Exchange (CBOE) are also launching perpetual contracts or similar products by the end of 2025.

In the Pax Americana retail space, calls for both ordinary citizens and the elite to trade perpetual contracts without VPNs prompted Coinbase to launch a "knock-off" version of perpetual contracts similar to the CBOE version earlier this year. In the waning Chicago Mercantile Exchange (CME) empire, other entrepreneurs daring to challenge its monopoly will have the opportunity to showcase their skills in a proposed regulatory sandbox managed by the U.S. Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC). It is said that after cracking down on myself and others in the crypto derivatives space, the CFTC appears to have changed its tune and is once again a supporter of financial innovation. What exactly has made perpetual contracts (PERPs) a "fight or die" moment for traditional finance (TradFi)? Why is the trading volume of derivatives for all financial assets globally shifting from expiring futures and options contracts to perpetual contracts that never expire? To answer these questions, I will first review the history of perpetual contracts, explaining how BitMEX and I invented this new type of financial instrument. Then, I will delve into the fundamental principles of perpetual contracts and why they are better suited to the 24/7 internet world. Finally, I will look at the prospects for the widespread adoption of perpetual contracts beyond the cryptocurrency space and explain why traditional exchanges must adjust their product lines to include perpetual contracts and shared loss margin systems, or they will soon be replaced by centralized exchanges (CEXs) and decentralized exchanges (DEXs). Hyperliquid, the hot new decentralized exchange (DEX), launched the permissionless protocol HIP-3, allowing a company called XYZ to create perpetual contracts for Nasdaq 100 index stocks. This contract currently has a daily trading volume exceeding $100 million, making it one of the largest contracts traded by ADV on Hyperliquid. Stock perpetual contracts are poised to become the hottest product of 2026, with all DEXs and centralized exchanges (CEXs), including BitMEX, offering such products by the end of next year. This product will revolutionize the stock perpetual derivatives trading market, which has so far lagged behind in terms of market access and competition for diversified trading venues. If you invest in exchange tokens or stocks, then you should know everything about perpetual contracts.

Understanding History

If you want to conquer a foreign people, strip them of their history. When I was a child, my parents always supplemented the official narrative taught in school with other books. This wasn't because they had some kind of established, alternative version of known facts; rather, they encouraged me to question the official narrative and recommended books with various perspectives. Clearly, much of this academic research stemmed from the lack of positive, truthful narratives about "Black" identity in American history. And my parents would never allow me to become ignorant as the public and private school education systems expected.

Based on this, I offer firsthand information about the history of perpetual contracts.

Recently, many people have jumped in to comment on the importance and origins of cryptocurrency perpetual contracts (often simply called "perpetual contracts"). Given that they often tell my story with BitMEX, and that it's usually wrong, let's clarify some facts. Like many things in life, the BitMEX team drew inspiration from others when creating perpetual contracts and their associated margin system. I'm furious if anyone belittles our team's achievements, claiming it's just a design by Robert Shiller from the 1990s that was later popularized by BitMEX and the cryptocurrency industry. Screw that! That's pure nonsense. Read on to find out why. Let's go back to May 2016, when BitMEX was a five-person company. Ben Delo and Sam Reed were my two co-founders, and our initial two employees were Greg Dwyer (Business Development) and Jinming Shao (Trading Engine Engineer). The cryptocurrency derivatives market landscape then had both similarities and differences to today. OKCoin (now OKX) and Huobi (now HTX) dominated the cryptocurrency derivatives market. Together, these two exchanges held approximately 95% of the market share (by daily trading volume). BitMEX ranked third, which, frankly, was negligible. Despite having the highest leverage of any exchange (100x), the liquidity of our futures contracts paled in comparison to OKCoin and Huobi. At the time, the most liquid contract was a quarterly futures contract with Bitcoin margin. At BitMEX, we experimented with various methods to improve contract liquidity. We launched calendar spread contracts, daily contracts, weekly contracts, monthly contracts, and quarterly contracts. In short, we spread liquidity across various XBT/USD futures contracts, resulting in excessively thin liquidity. We often asked ourselves if there was a way to create a product without an expiration date, thus concentrating liquidity on a single contract. We also continuously considered how to simplify derivatives trading to better attract users transitioning from margin trading to futures trading. Margin trading is easy to understand because traders simply borrow funds and then trade on the spot order book. Understanding expiration dates and the price difference or basis between futures and spot is completely unnecessary for margin traders. However, these traders become confused when transitioning to futures trading. Since Ben, Sam, and I personally answer all customer support questions, we always try to reduce our daily workload to handle other tasks. Developing a product that looks and operates similarly to margin trading would save us the trouble of answering many confusing customer questions. The initial version of the perpetual contract was born to solve these two problems. Ben and I contacted several BitMEX staunch supporters in Hong Kong, all of whom were excellent financial engineers. We asked them (Joseph Wang and Bhavik Patel) for ideas on how to develop such a product. The final solution was very similar to the perpetual futures contract mentioned in a paper published by Robert Shiller in 1993. Our first perpetual contract product was based on daily updated USD and XBT funding rates, allowing long and short positions to exchange future cash flows. At the time, the mainstream exchange for cryptocurrency spot and margin trading was Bitfinex, which offered a highly liquid P2P lending market. Users could borrow and lend through these markets, which directly affected the interest rates paid by margin traders. This was the design concept behind our first perpetual contract launched in May 2016. The margin system used an insurance fund and combined it with a socialized loss mechanism called "dynamic profit equilibration"; we jokingly referred to internally as a "double penetration experience." We launched the XBTUSD perpetual contract, delisted the daily and monthly Bitcoin/USD futures, but retained the quarterly futures contract. Most clients were confused by this, and our support tickets skyrocketed. One of the most confusing issues for traders was the funding rate. They didn't understand the calculation method. Clients bombarded us on forums, our trollbox, and through support tickets. They demanded that we remove the culprit and follow OKCoin's example by offering the most liquid quarterly futures contracts. Internal divisions also emerged within the company: some advocated retaining existing products, while others championed reverting to offering only futures contracts. The chaotic situation was bad enough, but the rapid rise in Bitcoin prices presented another problem. The success of the Shiller model requires sufficiently dynamic domestic and foreign interest rates to influence market behavior. If these rates are not sensitive enough to market reactions, trading prices will exhibit significant discounts or premiums relative to spot prices—the so-called basis. In 2016, Bitcoin prices surged, making it difficult to find short sellers. In other words, there was a shortage of synthetic dollars used to purchase spot and leveraged Bitcoin on exchanges. Therefore, we needed to find ways to increase the supply of synthetic dollars on BitMEX. The observed market interest rate differential between the dollar and Bitcoin in the Shiller model can achieve this. If Bitcoin rises rapidly in dollar terms, the dollar interest rate should be higher than the Bitcoin interest rate. On the Bitfinex exchange, the daily yield of the dollar is 1%, while the yield of Bitcoin is much lower. This is indeed a significant difference, but when Bitcoin's daily gains reach 10% to 25%, the gains are not enough to attract arbitrageurs to engage in synthetic lending by selling USD and buying Bitcoin in a market-neutral manner. Since the interest rates for USD and Bitcoin are provided by external platforms, BitMEX cannot control these rates. Therefore, our swap prices have a high and rising premium relative to the spot price. The problem with an excessively large basis between the printed line and the printed point is that it causes problems with the margin system. To illustrate this, I will provide a simplified and extreme example. XBTUSD (Perpetual Contract) = $1000; XBT (Spot) = $500. Base Price = $500. For traders, you can choose to mark your position at the current underlying price or the spot price. If marked at the spot price, the long position will have $500 of unrealized loss, and the short position will have $500 of unrealized profit. To ensure long positions meet margin requirements, we must charge an additional $500 margin. If the price is marked at the spot price, long positions don't need to pay this extra margin, but if the spot price quickly falls back to the spot price, long positions will lose $500, and short positions won't profit. To avoid this shared loss, we choose to mark traders at the spot price. This results in higher margin requirements for long positions. This is not ideal, as it makes trading costs too high compared to competitors who don't require extra margin from long positions. I recognized our problem and began looking for solutions, but market conditions took time to allow for implementation. This market failure validated the views of those internally questioning whether we should continue offering futures contracts. I firmly believed this product was the future and savior of our exchange, but I had to find a solution, otherwise negative feedback would force us back to futures. My solution was to create a backtesting index that records the basis between swap contracts and spot contracts. Under certain constraints, this basis would become the funding rate for the next period. For example, if the average premium of a futures contract over the past eight hours is 1% of the spot contract, then a long position held at the funding timestamp will pay a 1% commission to a short position. To prevent immediate liquidation at the funding arrival timestamps (UTC 12:00, UTC 20:00, UTC 04:00), we use the following function to automatically rebalance unrealized and realized profits and losses and limit the funding arrival rate: Minimum (Funding Rate, 75% * Maintenance Margin or 0.5%) To adjust the sensitivity of the funding rate to the premium index, simply shorten or lengthen the funding rate settlement cycle. For example, if the funding rate is charged hourly, the maximum cumulative funding rate paid daily is 0.35% * 12 = 4.20% per day. Ben, Jinming, and I spent a week researching how to implement this new funding calculation method from the perspective of trading engines and market structure. Ben and Jinming quickly added the feature to our trading engine. The feature was ready, but extreme market malfunction forced us to launch it. Our terrible market performance was causing great dissatisfaction among our clients. Sam wanted to cancel swap trading, but I convinced him that if we launched a premium index, the market would recover on its own within days. For better or worse, BitMEX wasn't an Arthur Hayes-style dictatorship, but rather a consensus-managed team of Arthur, Ben, and Sam. I secured a stay of execution for the culprits and set about fixing the market in typical Arthur Hayes style. My strength wasn't subtlety, so I decided to implement a sudden change over a weekend in mid-June 2016, which would be the most effective course of action. I announced that in 24 hours, the funding mechanism would be changed to a retrospective premium index. At the time, perpetual contracts were trading at a high premium, but after the change was announced, long positions were liquidated as anticipation of large-scale funding was high, causing perpetual contract prices to immediately fall. Because the perpetual contract price was trading at a significant premium to the spot price, financing payments would increase substantially. By Monday morning, the perpetual contract price had become much closer to the spot price, and the market imbalance had eased. I told Sam to observe for another day; due to the impact of high interest rates, the basis would reach its lowest point. On Monday, when Ben, Jinming, and I went to the Lai Chi Kok office for snacks, the perpetual contract price had begun to stabilize. Fortunately, my prediction about establishing a self-correcting mechanism to adjust financing amounts based on the previous basis worked, and the calls within the company to cancel perpetual contracts disappeared. The only major subsequent change to the product design was the introduction of Automatic Deleveraging (ADL) functionality. We borrowed from Huobi's ADL mechanism but made some adjustments. The biggest problem with Huobi's ADL implementation was that traders could not predict the probability of their positions being liquidated prematurely, and once liquidated by ADL, traders would lose their entire position. Therefore, our innovation lay in creating a ranking system based on several factors and allowing partial ADL. The front-end interface displays a heat bar showing traders the probability of their positions being liquidated by ADL. Some ADL functions allow the exchange to liquidate a portion of positions, thus balancing the profits and losses of both long and short positions.

Over the next few months, we gradually discontinued all Bitcoin futures contracts, followed by other cryptocurrency futures contracts. Traders gradually became familiar with the trading style and began to enjoy the product. One day in late 2017, BitMEX surpassed OKCoin to become the most liquid derivatives exchange. By the end of 2018, BitMEX had become the world's largest cryptocurrency exchange in terms of spot and derivatives trading volume.

Everyone stands on the shoulders of giants. I want to list the existing technologies that enabled the birth of perpetual contracts as a sign of due respect.

ICBIT:

This was the first cryptocurrency derivatives exchange. They invented the inverse futures contract. This means that the margin and profit/loss currency (Bitcoin) are the same as the native currency. For example, each contract is worth $100 of Bitcoin at any price.

Regardless of the Bitcoin price, the contract's USD exposure is always $100. In Bitcoin terms, the exposure is 1/x. This is an unusual derivative, but it works because the exchange only accepts Bitcoin deposits. It was here that I first traded Bitcoin futures and simulated an inverse contract on BitMEX based on their design. Robert Shiller: As I explained earlier, he wrote a paper on perpetual futures contracts in 1993. The interest payments exchanged between long and short positions are determined by an exogenous pricing factor. I've never read his paper, but I believe his ideas influenced the advice we received from Joseph Wang and Bavic Patel. China Commodity Exchange: The concepts of socialized loss, ADL (anti-fraud loss), and insurance funds all originated here. 796 was the first Chinese cryptocurrency derivatives exchange to adopt a socialized loss mechanism. In the industry, futures are "delta 1" products. During my brief career in traditional finance, I was a delta 1 trader. To truly understand why perpetual contracts are so transformative, it's essential to delve into how margin systems work in traditional finance and cryptocurrencies. One reason traditional financial exchanges can't offer high leverage to retail investors is that their clearinghouses handle settlements. If a losing party goes bankrupt, the clearinghouse must have sufficient paid-up capital to pay winning parties. Therefore, exchanges will pursue all assets of any bankrupt trader through legal means. However, the situation is quite different in the cryptocurrency space. Cryptocurrencies are extremely volatile. This volatility, coupled with high leverage, makes large-scale liquidations highly likely. For us cryptocurrency exchange owners, recovering cryptocurrencies through legal means is impractical because the cryptocurrencies we must recover are bearer assets. In traditional finance, you don't own your financial assets; these assets are held by intermediaries. Therefore, courts can easily instruct banks (as government agencies, banks will always obey court rulings) to send your assets to traditional financial exchanges to repay your debts. Courts cannot instruct the Bitcoin blockchain to send Bitcoin from one address to another. Therefore, the margin system of cryptocurrency exchanges can only rely on initial margin to cover liquidation fees. Thus, a socialized loss mechanism combined with an insurance fund is absolutely necessary for the operation of cryptocurrency derivatives exchanges. Another reason traditional trading platforms cannot offer 100x leverage is the severe lack of collateral in their clearinghouses. A few years ago, I did some research on the market capitalization of the world's largest clearinghouses, and the results were shocking. Given that the cryptocurrency market operates 24/7, governments and central banks cannot manipulate it like they do with the stock, bond, and foreign exchange markets. For example, large fluctuations in Bitcoin prices, coupled with a large number of open contracts in the derivatives market, could destroy the world's largest exchanges. Therefore, the margin levels offered by traditional trading platforms for cryptocurrencies and other derivatives are far lower than those of their native cryptocurrency competitors. This is crucial when comparing the high-leverage (20x) stock futures contracts offered by Hyperliquid and BitMEX (coming soon) with the low-leverage stock futures contracts offered by other trading platforms. Retail investors will abandon stock derivatives on trading platforms in favor of the high-leverage stock futures products offered by cryptocurrency exchanges. The only way for traditional trading platforms to compete is to change the clearing model of the entire global derivatives market, but I don't think that will happen in the short term. BitMEX's leverage history is a good example. BitMEX initially used a guaranteed settlement margin system. Therefore, we could only offer 3x leverage. Compared to our Chinese competitors, insufficient leverage was the main reason why our trading volume was almost zero for the first nine months of operation. To compete, I changed the system to a loss-sharing system, combined with our advanced trading engine technology, and by October 2015, the leverage ratio had increased to 100x. Shortly thereafter, our trading volume soared, and the exchange became profitable. Cryptocurrency derivatives exchanges offer clients high leverage they can't get in traditional finance (TradFi), but at the cost of sometimes missing out on full profits if prices fluctuate too quickly. This is ideal for cryptocurrencies because without perpetual contracts, the cost of leverage using options would be prohibitively high. A 10% increase in BTC in a single day means your unrealized profit is 10 BTC, or 10x return on equity. Trading options on highly volatile assets, even if the trade is correct, yields far less profit than trading highly leveraged physical stocks. Therefore, traders typically use physical stocks rather than options to gain leveraged exposure. Next, let's look at the next L – liquidity.
